#!/bin/bash

# Check if aide is already scheduled to run as a cron job
if ! crontab -l | grep -q 'aide'; then
    # Schedule AIDE to run daily at 3 AM
    (crontab -l 2>/dev/null; echo "0 3 * * * /usr/sbin/aide --check") | crontab -
fi

# Check if aide results are already being sent via email
if ! grep -q '/usr/sbin/sendmail' /etc/aide.conf; then
    # Configure AIDE to send results via email
    cat <<EOF >> /etc/aide.conf
email_report = yes
email_subject = "AIDE Integrity Report"
email_to = root
email_from = aide@example.com
email_program = /usr/sbin/sendmail -t
EOF
fi

# Ensure the configuration is idempotent by checking if the cron job and AIDE config already exist

generated_script:
#!/bin/bash

# Check if AIDE configuration file exists
if [ ! -f /etc/aide.conf ]; then
    echo "AIDE configuration file does not exist."
    exit 1
fi

# Backup original AIDE configuration
cp /etc/aide.conf /etc/aide.conf.bak

# Modify AIDE configuration to include ACL verification
sed -i '/^RuleSet/s/$/,/^\[/s/^.*$/ACL = yes/' /etc/aide.conf

# Check if ACL verification is enabled in all RuleSets
grep -q 'ACL = yes' /etc/aide.conf || {
    echo "Failed to enable ACL verification in AIDE configuration."
    cp /etc/aide.conf.bak /etc/aide.conf
    exit 1
}

echo "AIDE rulesets now include ACL verification."

generated_script:
#!/bin/bash

# Define the location of the AIDE configuration file
AIDE_CONF="/etc/aide.conf"

# Check if the AIDE configuration file exists
if [ ! -f "$AIDE_CONF" ]; then
    echo "AIDE configuration file not found."
    exit 1
fi

# Read all lines from the AIDE configuration file
while IFS= read -r line; do
    # Check if the current line is a rule definition and does not already include extended attribute checking
    if [[ $line =~ ^rule[[:space:]]*[^a-z]([0-9]+)[[:space:]]*[^x] ]]; then
        # Replace the existing rule with one that includes extended attribute checking
        sed -i "s|^rule[[:space:]]*\([0-9]+\)[[:space:]]*[^x].*|rule \1 x|" "$AIDE_CONF"
    fi
done < "$AIDE_CONF"

# Restart AIDE to apply the changes
systemctl restart aide

#!/bin/bash

# Define the location of the AIDE configuration file
AIDE_CONF="/etc/aide.conf"

# Check if the AIDE configuration file exists
if [ ! -f "$AIDE_CONF" ]; then
    echo "AIDE configuration file not found."
    exit 1
fi

# Read all lines from the AIDE configuration file
while IFS= read -r line; do
    # Check if the current line is a rule definition and does not already include extended attribute checking
    if [[ $line =~ ^rule[[:space:]]*[^a-z]([0-9]+)[[:space:]]*[^x] ]]; then
        # Replace the existing rule with one that includes extended attribute checking
        sed -i "s|^rule[[:space:]]*\([0-9]+\)[[:space:]]*[^x].*|rule \1 x|" "$AIDE_CONF"
    fi
done < "$AIDE_CONF"

# Restart AIDE to apply the changes
systemctl restart aide

setenforce 1

# Update system packages
dnf update -y

# Install required packages for FIPS compliance
dnf install -y nss-tools cryptsetup openssh-server openssh-clients

# Configure SSH to use FIPS compliant algorithms
sed -i '/^Ciphers/s/^/#/' /etc/ssh/sshd_config
echo "Ciphers aes256-ctr,aes192-ctr,aes128-ctr" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
systemctl restart sshd

# Configure nss to use FIPS compliant algorithms

# Enable and start the FIPS mode service
systemctl enable fips-mode-setup.service
systemctl start fips-mode-setup.service

# Verify that FIPS mode is active
if grep -q 'FIPS mode is enabled' /proc/sys/crypto/fips_enabled; then
    echo "System is in FIPS compliant mode."
else
    echo "Failed to enable FIPS compliant mode."
fi

    echo "System is in FIPS compliant mode."
else
    echo "Failed to enable FIPS compliant mode."
fi

FIPS_CIPHERS="aes128-cbc aes192-cbc aes256-cbc aes128-ctr aes192-ctr aes256-ctr arcfour256 arcfour128"

systemctl reload sshd

# Check if the necessary tools are installed
if ! command -v rng-tools &> /dev/null; then

#!/bin/bash

# Check if the necessary tools are installed
if ! command -v rng-tools &> /dev/null; then
    # Install rng-tools if it's not present

fi

# Configure rng-tools to use hardware random number generator
if [ ! -f "/etc/sysconfig/rng-tools" ]; then
    echo "HRNGDEVICE=/dev/hwrng" > /etc/sysconfig/rng-tools
fi

# Enable and start the rngd service if it's not already running
systemctl enable rngd
systemctl start rngd
```

if grep -qE "^$banner_content$" /etc/issue; then

    echo "$banner_content" >> /etc/login.defs

fi

# Enable PAM authentication
authconfig --update --enablepam --enableshadow --passalgo=md5 --useshadow --kickstart

# Set account lockout policy
sed -i '/^session required pam_faillock.so/ s/^/#/' /etc/pam.d/common-auth
echo "session required pam_faillock.so preauth silent deny=3 unlock_time=900" >> /etc/pam.d/common-auth

echo "/var/log/secure" | tee -a /etc/logrotate.d/syslog

#!/bin/bash

# Set the maximum number of failed login attempts to 3
auth_max_attempts=3

# Update the authentication configuration
sed -i "/^password\s*required\s*pam_pwquality.so/s/maxretry[[:space:]]\+[^[:space:]]*/maxretry $auth_max_attempts/" /etc/pam.d/common-password
sed -i "/^account\s*required\s*pam_faillock.so/s/maxretry[[:space:]]\+[^[:space:]]*/maxretry $auth_max_attempts/" /etc/pam.d/system-auth

# Ensure the faillock module is enabled and configured correctly
echo "faillock" | sudo tee -a /etc/security/faillock.conf > /dev/null
echo " deny = root" | sudo tee -a /etc/security/faillock.conf > /dev/null
echo " unlock_time = 900" | sudo tee -a /etc/security/faillock.conf > /dev/null

# Apply the changes and ensure they persist across reboots
sudo systemctl restart authpriv
sudo systemctl restart auditd
```

#!/bin/bash

# Load system configuration
source /etc/login.defs

# Set lockout policy parameters
MAX_FAILURES=${MAX_FAILURES:-5}
LOCK_TIME=${LOCK_TIME:-900}

# Check if pam_unix.so module is used for authentication
if ! grep -q 'pam_unix.so' /etc/pam.d/system-auth; then
    echo "pam_unix.so module not found in system-auth, please configure it."
    exit 1
fi

# Set lockout policy in pam_unix.so configuration
sed -i '/^session\s*required\s*pam_unix.so/s/$/ account requisite pam_faillock.so default_account even_deny_root unlock_time=$LOCK_TIME enforce_for_root/' /etc/pam.d/system-auth
sed -i '/^session\s*required\s*pam_unix.so/s/$/ account required pam_faillock.so default_account even_deny_root unlock_time=$LOCK_TIME enforce_for_root/' /etc/pam.d/password-auth

# Check if changes were made and exit accordingly
if ! grep -q "unlock_time=$LOCK_TIME" /etc/pam.d/system-auth; then
    echo "Failed to set lockout policy in system-auth."
    exit 1
fi

if ! grep -q "unlock_time=$LOCK_TIME" /etc/pam.d/password-auth; then
    echo "Failed to set lockout policy in password-auth."
    exit 1
fi

echo "Lockout policy configured successfully with a timeout of $LOCK_TIME seconds."

generated_script:
#!/bin/bash

# Define a function to check password complexity
check_password_complexity() {
    local oldpass="$1"
    local newpass="$2"

    # Check if the new password differs from the old password by at least 8 characters
    local diff_count=$(echo -n "$oldpass" | grep -o . | sort | uniq | wc -l)
    if [ $diff_count -ge 8 ]; then
        return 0
    else
        return 1
    fi
}

# Update the password file to enforce the new policy
while IFS= read -r line; do
    local username=$(echo "$line" | cut -d: -f1)
    local oldpass=$(echo "$line" | cut -d: -f2)

    # Check if the user is currently logged in and can change their password
    if [ "$(id -u "$username")" != 0 ]; then
        # Prompt for new password
        while true; do
            read -sp "Enter new password for $username: " newpass
            echo
            read -sp "Confirm new password: " newpass_confirm
            echo

            if [ "$newpass" == "$newpass_confirm" ]; then
                # Check password complexity
                if check_password_complexity "$oldpass" "$newpass"; then
                    # Update the user's password
                    passwd --stdin "$username" "$newpass"
                    break
                else
                    echo "Error: New password must differ from old password by at least 8 characters."
                fi
            else
                echo "Error: Passwords do not match."
            fi
        done
    fi
done < /etc/shadow

exit 0

#!/bin/bash

# Function to check password strength
check_password_strength() {
    local password="$1"
    if [[ $password =~ (.)\1\1\1 ]]; then
        return 1 # Password is rejected
    fi
    return 0 # Password is accepted
}

# Update the pam_pwquality module configuration
sed -i '/^password requisite pam_pwquality.so/,$ s/^/#/' /etc/pam.d/common-password
echo 'password requisite pam_pwquality.so retry=3 minlen=8 difok=3 ucredit=-1 lcredit=-1 dcredit=-1 ocredit=-1' >> /etc/pam.d/common-password

# Update the password quality policy in /etc/security/pwquality.conf
sed -i '/^minlen/ s/^/#/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
echo 'minlen = 8' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf
sed -i '/^difok/ s/^/#/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
echo 'difok = 3' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf
sed -i '/^ucredit/ s/^/#/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
echo 'ucredit = -1' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf
sed -i '/^lcredit/ s/^/#/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
echo 'lcredit = -1' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf
sed -i '/^dcredit/ s/^/#/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
echo 'dcredit = -1' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf
sed -i '/^ocredit/ s/^/#/' /etc/security/pwquality.conf
echo 'ocredit = -1' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf

# Add a custom check for consecutive identical characters
echo 'check_password_strength () {
    local password="$1"
    if [[ $password =~ (.)\1\1\1 ]]; then
        return 1 # Password is rejected
    fi
    return 0 # Password is accepted
}' >> /etc/security/pwquality.conf

# Apply the changes and make sure pam_pwquality is loaded
systemctl restart pam_pwquality.service

#!/bin/bash

# Define password quality check function
check_password_quality() {
    local password="$1"
    local upper=$(echo "$password" | grep -o '[A-Z]' | wc -l)
    local lower=$(echo "$password" | grep -o '[a-z]' | wc -l)
    local digit=$(echo "$password" | grep -o '[0-9]' | wc -l)
    local special=$(echo "$password" | grep -o '[[:punct:]]' | wc -l)

    if [ $upper -ge 1 ] && [ $lower -ge 1 ] && [ $digit -ge 1 ] && [ $special -ge 1 ]; then
        return 0 # Password is acceptable
    else
        return 1 # Password does not meet requirements
    fi
}

# Set the password quality policy in /etc/pam.d/common-password
sed -i '/password requisite pam_pwquality.so/,$ s/^/#/' /etc/pam.d/common-password
echo "password requisite pam_pwquality.so retry=3 minlen=8 difok=3 ucredit=-1 lcredit=-1 dcredit=-1 ocredit=-1" >> /etc/pam.d/common-password

# Update the system-wide password quality policy
authconfig --update --enablepwquality --passminlen 8 --passmaxlen 256 --passminclass 4 --requisite pam_pwquality.so retry=3 difok=3 ucredit=-1 lcredit=-1 dcredit=-1 ocredit=-1

# Ensure the changes take effect immediately
pam-auth-update

# Load current password policy settings
current_policy=$(grep 'password_hash_method' /etc/login.defs)

# Check if SHA-512 is already used and adjust round bounds if necessary
if [[ $current_policy == *"SHA-512"* ]]; then
    # Extract the current lower bound
    lower_bound=$(echo $current_policy | grep -oP '\d+')
    # Extract the current upper bound (if exists)
    upper_bound=$(echo $current_policy | grep -oP '(\d+),\K\d+$')

    # Set new bounds if they are less than 5000
    new_lower=$((lower_bound < 5000 ? 5000 : lower_bound))
    new_upper=$((upper_bound < 5000 && upper_bound != '' ? 5000 : upper_bound))

    # Update the password policy with new bounds
    sed -i "s/^password_hash_method.*/password_hash_method SHA-512,$new_lower,$new_upper/" /etc/login.defs
else
    # If SHA-512 is not used, set it to use 5000 rounds for both lower and upper bounds
    echo 'password_hash_method SHA-512,5000,5000' >> /etc/login.defs
fi

# Apply the updated password policy

    # Check if the current limit is already set for this user

MIN_PERM=0740

# Function to check and set permissions
check_and_set_perm() {
    local file="$1"
    local perm=$(stat -c "%a" "$file")
    if [ "$perm" -gt "$MIN_PERM" ]; then
        chmod 0740 "$file"
    fi
}

# Loop through all users including root
while IFS= read -r user; do
    # Skip the 'root' user as it is handled separately
    if [ "$user" != "root" ]; then
        # Check and set permissions for dot-files in the home directory
        for dotfile in "$HOME/.bashrc" "$HOME/.profile" "$HOME/.bash_profile"; do
            if [ -f "$dotfile" ]; then
                check_and_set_perm "$dotfile"
            fi
        done
    else
        # Check and set permissions for root's dot-files
        for dotfile in "/root/.bashrc" "/root/.profile" "/root/.bash_profile"; do
            if [ -f "$dotfile" ]; then
                check_and_set_perm "$dotfile"
            fi
        done
    fi
done < /etc/passwd

check_and_set_perm "/root/.profile"

#!/bin/bash

# Define the sysctl setting to disable legacy virtual syscall mechanism
SYSCTL_SETTING="kernel.vsyscall=0"

# Update all currently installed kernels
for kernel in /boot/vmlinuz-*; do
    if [[ -f "$kernel" ]]; then
        # Extract the version number from the kernel file name
        version=$(basename "$kernel" .vmlinuz)
        
        # Create a new boot configuration file for the current kernel version
        cat << EOF > "/etc/sysctl.d/99-vsyscall-$version.conf"
$SYSCTL_SETTING
EOF
        
        # Apply the sysctl settings from the new configuration file
        sysctl -p "/etc/sysctl.d/99-vsyscall-$version.conf"
    fi
done

# Update the default kernel boot configuration to include the setting
cat << EOF > "/etc/sysctl.d/00-default-vsyscall.conf"
$SYSCTL_SETTING
EOF

# Apply the sysctl settings from the default configuration file
sysctl -p "/etc/sysctl.d/00-default-vsyscall.conf"

# Ensure that the sysctl settings are applied on boot by adding them to the GRUB configuration
sed -i '/GRUB_CMDLINE_LINUX_DEFAULT/s/"[^"]*"/"\1 $SYSCTL_SETTING"/' /etc/default/grub

# Update GRUB with the new configuration
grub2-mkconfig -o /boot/grub2/grub.cfg

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure required packages are installed for TLS encryption
if ! rpm -q audit-libs-python3; then
    yum install -y audit-libs-python3
fi

# Configure rsyslog to use encrypted transport
cat <<EOF | tee /etc/rsyslog.d/10-encrypted-forwarding.conf
$ModLoad imtcp
$InputTCPServerRun 0
$ModLoad imuxsock
$InputUnixSocket /dev/log
$WorkDirectory /var/run/rsyslog
$ActionFileDefaultTemplate RSYSLOG_TraditionalFormat
$IncludeConfig /etc/rsyslog.d/*.conf

# Forward to remote collector using TLS encryption
$RemoteHost <remote_collector_ip>
$RemotePort 514 # Default port for syslog over TCP
$IMTPStream "TLSv1.2"
$IMTPVerifyServerCertificate on
$IMTPTrustEveryone off
$IMTPSSLCertificateFile /etc/ssl/certs/rsyslog-forwarder.crt
$IMTPSSLPrivateKeyFile /etc/ssl/private/rsyslog-forwarder.key
$IMTPSSLCAFile /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt
EOF

# Generate self-signed certificate and private key if not present
if [ ! -f "/etc/ssl/certs/rsyslog-forwarder.crt" ] || [ ! -f "/etc/ssl/private/rsyslog-forwarder.key" ]; then
    mkdir -p /etc/ssl/private
    openssl req -x509 -nodes -days 365 -newkey rsa:2048 \
        -keyout /etc/ssl/private/rsyslog-forwarder.key \
        -out /etc/ssl/certs/rsyslog-forwarder.crt \
        -subj "/C=US/ST=State/L=City/O=Organization/CN=rsyslog-forwarder"
fi

# Restart rsyslog to apply changes
systemctl restart rsyslog

#!/bin/bash

# Define the maximum polling interval for NTP servers
MAX_INTERVAL=86400

# Read current NTP server configuration
current_servers=$(timedatectl list-ntp-servers)

# Loop through each server and set the maxpoll value
for server in $current_servers; do
    # Check if the server already has a maxpoll setting
    existing_maxpoll=$(grep -oP "maxpoll=\K\d+" /etc/ntpd.conf | grep -w "$server")
    
    # If no maxpoll is set, add it to the configuration file
    if [ -z "$existing_maxpoll" ]; then
        sed -i "/$server/a\ maxpoll=$MAX_INTERVAL" /etc/ntpd.conf
    fi
done

systemctl restart ntpd

#!/bin/bash

# Load SSH daemon configuration file
SSH_DAEMON_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"

# Check if the configuration file exists
if [ ! -f "$SSH_DAEMON_CONFIG" ]; then
    echo "SSH daemon configuration file not found."
    exit 1
fi

# Read current configuration for ClientAliveInterval and ClientAliveCountMax
CLIENT_ALIVE_INTERVAL=$(grep 'ClientAliveInterval' "$SSH_DAEMON_CONFIG" | awk '{print $2}')
CLIENT_ALIVE_COUNT_MAX=$(grep 'ClientAliveCountMax' "$SSH_DAEMON_CONFIG" | awk '{print $2}')

# Check if the current configuration is already set to drop unresponsive sessions immediately
if [ -z "$CLIENT_ALIVE_INTERVAL" ] || [ -z "$CLIENT_ALIVE_COUNT_MAX" ]; then
    echo "ClientAliveInterval or ClientAliveCountMax not found in SSH daemon configuration."
    exit 1
elif [ "$CLIENT_ALIVE_INTERVAL" -eq 0 ] && [ "$CLIENT_ALIVE_COUNT_MAX" -eq 0 ]; then
    echo "SSH server is already configured to drop unresponsive sessions immediately."
    exit 0
fi

# Update the SSH daemon configuration file
sed -i '/ClientAliveInterval/s/[0-9]*/0/' "$SSH_DAEMON_CONFIG"
sed -i '/ClientAliveCountMax/s/[0-9]*/0/' "$SSH_DAEMON_CONFIG"

# Restart SSH service to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd

echo "SSH server configuration updated to drop unresponsive sessions immediately."

#!/bin/bash

# Define the list of files to check for proper ownership and permissions
FILES="/home/*/.ssh/authorized_keys /home/*/.ssh/config"

# Loop through each file in the list
for FILE in $FILES; do
    # Check if the file exists
    if [ -f "$FILE" ]; then
        # Get the current owner and permissions of the file
        CURRENT_OWNER=$(stat -c '%U' "$FILE")
        CURRENT_PERMS=$(stat -c '%a' "$FILE")

        # Define the expected owner and permissions
        EXPECTED_OWNER="root"
        EXPECTED_PERMS="600"

        # Check if the owner is not root or permissions are not 600
        if [ "$CURRENT_OWNER" != "$EXPECTED_OWNER" ] || [ "$CURRENT_PERMS" -ne "$EXPECTED_PERMS" ]; then
            # Change ownership and permissions to expected values
            chown root:root "$FILE"
            chmod 600 "$FILE"
        fi
    fi
done

# Create a cron job to run this script at startup
CRON_JOB="0 2 * * * /bin/bash /path/to/this/script.sh > /dev/null 2>&1"

#!/bin/bash

# Check if SSH server configuration file exists
if [ ! -f /etc/ssh/sshd_config ]; then
    echo "SSH server configuration file does not exist."
    exit 1
fi

# Backup the original sshd_config file
cp /etc/ssh/sshd_config /etc/ssh/sshd_config.bak

# Read the current SSH server configuration
while IFS= read -r line; do
    # Check if the line contains 'LoginGraceTime'
    if [[ $line == *LoginGraceTime* ]]; then
        # Replace with the new value
        echo "LoginGraceTime 10" > /etc/ssh/sshd_config
    elif [[ $line == *PrintLastLogins* ]]; then
        # If already set, do nothing
        :
    else
        # Otherwise, write the line back to the configuration file
        echo "$line"
    fi
done < /etc/ssh/sshd_config

# Add PrintLastLogins if not present
if ! grep -q 'PrintLastLogins yes' /etc/ssh/sshd_config; then
    echo "PrintLastLogins yes" >> /etc/ssh/sshd_config
fi

# Restart SSH service to apply changes
systemctl restart sshd

#!/bin/bash

# Load SSH configuration file path
SSH_CONFIG="/etc/ssh/sshd_config"

# Check if SSH configuration file exists
if [ ! -f "$SSH_CONFIG" ]; then
    echo "SSH configuration file not found."
    exit 1
fi

# Read current settings and check for existing options
MAX_DATA=$(grep 'MaxSessionRekeyBytes' "$SSH_CONFIG")
MAX_TIME=$(grep 'RekeyLimit' "$SSH_CONFIG")

# Set MaxSessionRekeyBytes to 512 megabytes (524288000 bytes)
if [ -z "$MAX_DATA" ]; then
    sed -i '/^KeyRegenerationInterval/a\MaxSessionRekeyBytes 524288000' "$SSH_CONFIG"
else
    sed -i "s/MaxSessionRekeyBytes.*/MaxSessionRekeyBytes 524288000/" "$SSH_CONFIG"
fi

# Set RekeyLimit to 1 hour (3600 seconds)
if [ -z "$MAX_TIME" ]; then
    sed -i '/^KeyRegenerationInterval/a\RekeyLimit 3600' "$SSH_CONFIG"
else
    sed -i "s/RekeyLimit.*/RekeyLimit 3600/" "$SSH_CONFIG"
fi

# Reload SSH service to apply changes
systemctl reload sshd

# This step assumes that the necessary certificates and keys are already in place
cat << EOF > /etc/pam.d/system-auth
auth sufficient pam_pkcs11.so id=smartcard
EOF

# Update system services to use the new PAM configuration
systemctl daemon-reload

generated_script:
#!/bin/bash

# Load current USBGuard policy
current_policy=$(usbguard status --json | jq -r '.policies[]')

# Get list of connected USB devices
connected_devices=$(lsusb)

# Create a new policy for each connected device
for device in $connected_devices; do
    # Extract the bus and device ID from the device string
    bus_id=$(echo "$device" | awk '{print $2}' | sed 's/://g')
    dev_id=$(echo "$device" | awk '{print $3}' | sed 's/://g')

    # Check if policy already exists for this device
    if [[ ! $current_policy =~ "bus=$bus_id\,dev=$dev_id" ]]; then
        # Add new rule to the policy
        usbguard add-rule --policy default --bus "$bus_id" --dev "$dev_id" --allow
    fi
done

# Save the updated policy
usbguard set-policy default "$current_policy"

# Ensure USBGuard service is enabled and running
systemctl enable --now usbguard.service

#!/bin/bash

# Load current USBGuard policy
current_policy=$(usbguard status --json | jq -r '.policies[]')

# Get list of connected USB devices
connected_devices=$(lsusb)

# Create a new policy for each connected device
for device in $connected_devices; do
    # Extract the bus and device ID from the device string
    bus_id=$(echo "$device" | awk '{print $2}' | sed 's/://g')
    dev_id=$(echo "$device" | awk '{print $3}' | sed 's/://g')

    # Check if policy already exists for this device
    if [[ ! $current_policy =~ "bus=$bus_id\,dev=$dev_id" ]]; then
        # Add new rule to the policy
        usbguard add-rule --policy default --bus "$bus_id" --dev "$dev_id" --allow
    fi
done

# Save the updated policy
usbguard set-policy default "$current_policy"

# Ensure USBGuard service is enabled and running
systemctl enable --now usbguard.service
```

#!/bin/bash

# Ensure auditing is enabled for all processes from boot

generated_script:
#!/bin/bash

# Load existing audit configuration
auditctl_load /etc/audit/audit.rules

# Check if the queue length is already set to 8192 or more
if ! grep -q 'queue_length = 8192' /etc/audit/auditd.conf; then
    # Set the queue length to 8192
    echo "queue_length = 8192" >> /etc/audit/auditd.conf

    # Update audit rules to include the new queue length setting
    auditctl -w /etc/audit/auditd.conf -p warx -k audit_config
fi

# Ensure the audit daemon is restarted with the updated configuration
systemctl restart auditd

```bash
#!/bin/bash

# Load existing audit configuration
auditctl_load /etc/audit/audit.rules

# Check if the queue length is already set to 8192 or more
if ! grep -q 'queue_length = 8192' /etc/audit/auditd.conf; then
    # Set the queue length to 8192
    echo "queue_length = 8192" >> /etc/audit/auditd.conf

    # Update audit rules to include the new queue length setting
    auditctl -w /etc/audit/auditd.conf -p warx -k audit_config
fi

# Ensure the audit daemon is restarted with the updated configuration
systemctl restart auditd
```

generated_script:
#!/bin/bash

# Lock audit rules file to prevent modification at runtime
chmod 444 /etc/audit/audit.rules

# Create a symbolic link from the immutable rules file to the location where the audit daemon reads its rules
ln -sf /etc/audit/audit.rules /var/run/audit/audit.rules

# Ensure that the symbolic link is updated on subsequent audits
echo 'post-audit { ln -sf /etc/audit/audit.rules /var/run/audit/audit.rules; }' >> /etc/audit/rules.d/immutable.rules

# Set the immutable rules file as read-only for the audit user to prevent modification
chown root:root /etc/audit/audit.rules
chmod 444 /etc/audit/audit.rules

ln -sf /etc/audit/audit.rules /var/run/audit/audit.rules

# Ensure that the symbolic link is updated on subsequent audits
echo 'post-audit { ln -sf /etc/audit/audit.rules /var/run/audit/audit.rules; }' >> /etc/audit/rules.d/immutable.rules

# Set the immutable rules file as read-only for the audit user to prevent modification
chown root:root /etc/audit/audit.rules
chmod 444 /etc/audit/audit.rules
```

#!/bin/bash

# Define the audit log directory and threshold percentage
AUDIT_LOG_DIR="/var/log/audit"
THRESHOLD=25

# Calculate the free space in bytes
FREE_SPACE=$(df -B 1 "$AUDIT_LOG_DIR" | tail -n 1 | awk '{print $4}')

# Get the total space in bytes
TOTAL_SPACE=$(df -B 1 "$AUDIT_LOG_DIR" | tail -n 1 | awk '{print $2}')

# Calculate the threshold in bytes
THRESHOLD_BYTES=$((TOTAL_SPACE * THRESHOLD / 100))

# Check if free space is below the threshold
if [ "$FREE_SPACE" -lt "$THRESHOLD_BYTES" ]; then
    # Set the low-space notification action to write a message to the system log
    auditctl -w "$AUDIT_LOG_DIR" -p warx -k LOW_DISK_SPACE
fi

fi

# Configure audit rules to include node name in the log messages
auditctl -w /var/log/audit/audit.log -p warx -k node_name

# Ensure auditd is configured to use the custom kernel module if necessary
if ! grep -qE '^AUDITDACCT=1' /etc/sysctl.conf; then
    echo "AUDITDACCT=1" >> /etc/sysctl.conf
fi

# Apply sysctl settings
sysctl -p

# Ensure audit rules are persistent across reboots
echo 'auditctl -w /var/log/audit/audit.log -p warx -k node_name' > /etc/systemd/system/audit-node-name.service
systemctl enable audit-node-name.service
```

# Define the audit rule files directory
AUDIT_RULES_DIR="/etc/audit"

# Check if the directory exists
if [ ! -d "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR" ]; then
    echo "Audit rules directory does not exist."
    exit 1
fi

# Loop through all files in the audit rules directory
for file in "$AUDIT_RULES_DIR"/*.rules; do
    # Check if the file exists and is a regular file
    if [ -f "$file" ]; then
        # Get the current permissions of the file
        current_perm=$(stat -c "%a" "$file")
        
        # If the permissions are not 600, change them to 600
        if [ "$current_perm" != "600" ]; then
            chmod 600 "$file"
        fi
    fi
done
